title: Slavery was nothing new
content: Slavery is as old as civilization. Egypt, Rome, China, the great African kingdoms, and even the pre-Columbian Americas all ran on unfree labor. For centuries Arab and Mediterranean merchants bought and sold people across half the world. The Atlantic trade did not invent slavery — it industrialized it.
next: If slavery was everywhere, why did it suddenly target Africa?

title: A line drawn by religion, not race
content: In medieval Europe the rule was simple: Christians didn't enslave Christians, and Muslims didn't enslave Muslims — but both enslaved outsiders. As the Reconquista and the rising Ottoman Empire cut off their usual non-Christian captives, Portugal and Spain ran short of labor and began sailing south along Africa's coast. In 1452 the Pope even granted them the right to enslave non-Christians 'in perpetuity.'
next: What turned a trickle of captives into an industry?

title: The Americas create a hunger for labor
content: After Columbus reached the Americas in 1492, Europeans built plantation colonies — then watched their forced indigenous workers die in huge numbers from European diseases. Crops like sugar demanded a vast, constant workforce. In 1526 the first recorded shipload of enslaved Africans crossed to Brazil, beginning a system that would last three centuries.
next: How did Europeans get hold of so many captives?

title: Tapping a system that already existed
content: Raiding the interior was costly and dangerous, so Europeans plugged into Africa's existing slave networks instead. They built fortified coastal trading posts called 'factories' and bought war captives from local rulers in exchange for cloth, iron, firearms, and alcohol. Millions would pass through these stone enclosures.
next: Who turned this into the biggest operation of all?

title: Britain takes over
content: The Dutch, French, and Spanish all joined in, but the British scaled it up. After a royal monopoly was thrown open to all merchants in 1698, private traders flooded the market. The port of Liverpool — backed by Britain's textile mills, gunsmiths, and new docks — came to control most of the entire Atlantic trade.
next: How did one voyage actually work?

title: The triangular trade
content: One voyage, three legs, each turning a profit. Ships left Britain loaded with manufactured goods, traded them in Africa for enslaved people, carried those people across the ocean, then returned with sugar, cotton, and tobacco. The cycle then began again.
next: What was that ocean crossing like for the people below deck?

title: The Middle Passage
content: The crossing from Africa to the Americas took six to eight weeks. Below deck, each enslaved person had a space barely larger than a coffin, men chained in pairs. Disease spread fast and roughly one in seven died. Revolts were constant — yet captains packed ships tight, because crowding meant profit.
next: If it was this brutal, how was it all paid for?

title: Built like any other business
content: This was no chaotic crime — it was sophisticated finance. A single voyage was split into 64 shares owned by syndicates of ordinary investors: lawyers, clergymen, politicians. Lloyd's of London insured enslaved people as 'cargo,' and the Bank of England extended credit. The whole economy profited.
next: What finally turned people against it?

title: The Zong massacre
content: In 1781 the crew of the Liverpool ship Zong threw 132 enslaved people into the sea, then tried to claim them on insurance as lost 'cargo.' The court treated it as a question of property, not murder. The public outrage that followed gave the abolitionist movement its rallying cry.
next: Could outrage actually change the law?

title: Britain bans the trade
content: Campaigners — Quakers, the MP William Wilberforce, and formerly enslaved writers like Olaudah Equiano — built a mass movement and a boycott of slave-grown sugar. In 1807 Parliament banned British ships from carrying enslaved people. The Royal Navy then patrolled Africa's coast, freeing around 150,000 people from slave ships.
next: Did banning the trade actually free anyone?

title: Freedom — and its long shadow
content: Banning the trade was not the same as freeing the enslaved. That came in 1833 — but Britain paid compensation to the slave owners, not the people they had enslaved. The debt was so vast it was not fully repaid until 2015. The wealth and inequality the trade built still echo today.
